stimulus: When Paraguayan novelist Susana Britos, who had previously published three works in Spanish, began writing in her ancestral language, Guaraní, in the 1980s, several colleagues and reviewers warned that doing so might place her books out of reach of readers beyond her own region. Some pointed out that Portuguese — widely read across South America — would be a more practical alternative. Rejecting their advice, Britos went on to author more than a dozen acclaimed novels in Guaraní that have been translated into a total of over twenty languages.
stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

answerOptions.content: Although Britos’s decision to write in Guaraní was met with skepticism, it did not prevent her from achieving literary success.
Although Britos insisted on publishing her earliest works in Guaraní, they have since been translated into many other languages.
The reaction to Britos’s rejection of Spanish illustrates that some literary experts believe fame is most easily gained by writing in a widely read language, such as Guaraní.
In the 1980s, Britos became convinced that literature ought to be written in authors’ ancestral languages, and she proceeded to publish many works in Guaraní.

rationale:
Choice A is the best answer. The text says colleagues warned Britos that writing in Guaraní would limit her readership and that she nevertheless went on to publish more than a dozen acclaimed novels translated into over twenty languages — so the skepticism did not prevent her success.

Choice B is incorrect because the text states she had previously published three works in Spanish, so her earliest works were not in Guaraní. Choice C is incorrect because the text describes Guaraní as the language whose use raised concerns about reach, not as widely read. Choice D is incorrect because the text never says what motivated Britos’s choice or that she advocated for other authors to follow her example.
